paper_title: Comparing Linear Probes with Mahalanobis Cosine Similarity

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Linear probes are widely used in interpretability research and often compared by cosine similarity. The Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) between two directions, which reweights the inner product by test data covariance, is a natural task-aware refinement. Ying et al. (2026) report that a probe's MCS to a reference probe trained on the out-of-distribution (OOD) data near-perfectly linearly predicts the probe's OOD AUROC (R^2 = 0.98). Here, we extend this empirical finding across models, layers, and concept domains, and prove this general phenomenon in closed form: For balanced classes whose projections are Gaussian, OOD AUROC and MCS to the reference probe are linear because both are sigmoid-shaped functions of the probe's signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) on the test data. The theory also predicts when this linearity fails, which we verify empirically. MCS offers a theoretically grounded and empirically effective alternative to Euclidean cosine similarity for comparing linear probes.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Linear probes are widely used in interpretability research and often compared by cosine The Mahalanobis cosine simisimilarity. larity (MCS) between two directions, which reweights the inner product by test data covariance, is natural task-aware refinement. Ying et al. (2026) report that probes MCS to reference probe trained on the out-of-distribution (OOD) data near-perfectly linearly predicts the probes OOD AUROC (R2=0.98). Here, we extend this empirical finding across models, layers, and concept domains, and prove this general phenomenon in closed form: For balanced classes whose projections are Gaussian, OOD AUROC and MCS to the reference probe are linear because both are sigmoid-shaped functions of the probes signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) on the test data. The theory also predicts when this linearity fails, which we verify empirically. MCS offers theoretically grounded and empirically effective alternative to Euclidean cosine similarity for comparing linear probes.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Linear probes are powerful interpretability tools (Alain and Bengio, 2016; Belinkov, 2022; Marks and Tegmark, 2023), but their transfer behavior is notoriously brittle: they often fail to generalize to closely related datasets, and break dramatically under subtle shifts such as negation or prompt-format changes (Hewitt and Liang, 2019; Levinstein and Herrmann, 2024; Orgad et al., 2025). Understanding which probes generalize, and why two probes that look similar on one distribution diverge on another, requires principled way to compare them. Ying et al. (2026) report that the Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) between probe wid trained on an in-distribution (ID) task and another probe wood trained on an out-of-distribution (OOD) task, which reweights the inner product between the two probes by the OOD data covariance, is linearly related to the OOD AUROC of probe wid (R2=0.98). 1 However, this was shown only for one model on truthfulness datasets, and such near-perfect linearity demands theoretical explanation. Empirically, we replicate and substantially extend this regularity. The near-linear AUROC MCS relationship holds across models (Llama-70B, Llama-8B, Qwen-7B), across layers (2065 of Llama-70B), and across 24 datasets from three concept domains (truthfulness, gender classification, and general NLP benchmarks), with R2>0.93 in every condition. Euclidean cosine similarity (ECS), by contrast, drops to as low as R2=0.06 (2). Intuitively, ECS treats all dimensions equally, but only the dimensions where the data varies affect probe performance. MCS reweights the inner product by OOD data covariance, thus comparing probes in the subspace that actually matters. Theoretically, we prove why this linear relationship holds. Under balanced classes and per-class projection Gaussianity, both the ID probes OOD AUROC and its MCS to the OOD probe are Sshapedd functions of the ID probes signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) on the OOD task: AUROC is the Gaussian CDF Φ(s/ 2) in s, and MCS is softsign function that converges to s/(cid:112)(4 + s2) with moderately large Fisher distance zmax. Composing the two, where each S-shaped inverts the others curvature, cancels out the S-shapeds, leaving nearlinear AUROCMCS curve (3). The theory also predicts when the linearity fails: small zmax, class imbalance, non-Fisher reference directions like difference of means, or using pooled covariance instead of the total covariance. We verify each failure mode in simulations or on real activations (4). Most elements here are textbook (Fisher, 1936; Green et al., 1966; Chatfield, 2018), and MCS itself is not new (Bolme et al., 2003; Iacovacci et al., 2020). The closest theoretical neighbor is Agreement-on-the-Line (Miller et al., 2021; Baek et al., 2022, 2025), which uses the same covarianceprojected cosine to predict accuracy linearity beFigure 1: Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) linearly tracks generalization performance. (a) AUROC is near-linear function of MCS across heterogeneous tasks. (bc) The generalization AUROC heatmap and the MCS heatmap share structure almost entry-for-entry. Reproduced from Ying et al. (2026) Condition R2 (MCS) R2 (ECS) Llama-70B, L33, truth 0. 0.441 Layers (Llama-70B, truth) layer 20 layer 50 layer 65 Domains (Llama-70B, L33) Gender classification General NLP benchmarks Models (truth) Llama-3.1-8B Qwen2.5-7B 0.990 0.963 0.976 0.976 0.936 0.935 0.994 0.628 0.422 0.446 0.723 0.057 0.530 0. Table 1: Linear-fit R2 of held-out AUROC against Mahalanobis vs. standard Euclidean cosine similarity (MCS vs. ECS), across layers, domains, and model families and sizes. MCS dominates ECS in every condition. tween classifiers. Composing these ingredients into closed-form law relating MCS and OOD AUROC is novel. See App. for extended related works. Together, MCS offers theoretically grounded task-aware alternative for comparing linear probes. data covariance Σtot.1 We contrast against the pooled within-class covariance Σpool in 4. For each (ID, OOD) pair, we split the OOD data in half: on the train half, we train the probes and compute Σpool, Σtot, δ, wood, and SNR; on the disjoint test half, we compute the empirical AUROC of wid. This avoids bias caused by overfitting. Results. We show that the OOD AUROC of wid is near-linear function of MCSΣtot(wid, wood), with R20.93 for all conditions tested (Fig. 1a, Tab. 1). ECS is substantially worse: R2 drops to 0.06 in the worst condition. Fig. 1bc shows that the structure of the generalization-AUROC heatmap (every (train, test) pair on the truthfulness benchmarks) is mirrored almost entry-for-entry by the MCS heatmap. See Fig. 4, 5 and Tab. 2 in App. for the generalization performance, MCS and ECS plotted against AUROC, and robustness to Σtot estimators (full sample, LedoitWolf, and per-coordinate diagonal) across all conditions.

sections.title: 3 Theory
sections.content: Design. We use Llama-3.3-70B at residualstream layer 33, with mean-pooled activations over response tokens following Ying et al. (2026). To test generality, we additionally evaluate at layers {20, 50, 65} of Llama-3.3-70B, at middle layers of Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen2.5-7B (Qwen et al., 2025; Grattafiori et al., 2024). We use datasets across three domains exhibiting rich generalization patterns: ten truthfulness datasets, six gender classification datasets, and eight general NLP classification datasets. See App. for details. We train logistic-regression probes for each task. We denote the ID and OOD trained probe direction by wid and wood. For directions u, we define MCSM (u, v) := uM uM vM , (1) and instantiate with the full sample OOD train 2 3.1 Setup Let Rd be feature vector and {0, 1} binary label. We analyse the OOD behaviour of fixed direction wid. Assumption 1 (Moments). Under the OOD distribution, Pr(Y =0)= Pr(Y =1)= 1 2 , and the classconditional distributions have means µ0, µ1 and covariances Σ0, Σ1 0. Define δ := µ1µ0 and Σpool := 1 Assumption 2 (Projection Gaussianity). For each direction used in an AUROC statement below, the projection wX =c is Gaussian for {0, 1}. 2 (Σ0+Σ1). 1This is the opposite of the weighting in Mahalanobis distance (with Σ1), which measures distances between points. Since we instead care about distances between probe weights (dual space) rather than data points (primal space), our formulation transforms data and probes inversely and thus preserves projections onto the probe in the whitened space as desired. Figure 2: Theory predicts empirical data without free parameters. Across panels, empirical points largely lie on the theory prediction. (a) AUROCSNR shows Lemma 2. (b) MCSSNR shows Theorem 1. (c) Eliminating SNR, AUROCMCS shows near-straight line that bends only in the top-right corner, matching the empirical data. This is much weaker than joint Gaussianity of X, and plausible even for non-Gaussian distributions in high dimensional space by projection concentration (a DiaconisFreedman-style CLT for onedimensional projections). Empirically, the per-class projections are largely normal  (Fig. 7)  , and simulations show the linearity survives clearly non-Gaussian projections  (Fig. 8)  . Since the empirical projections are Gaussian, we retain this assumption for simplicity. For Rd {0} we define SNR(w) := wδ (cid:112)wΣpoolw , zmax := (cid:113) δΣ1 poolδ, (2) In this section, wood denotes the Fisher discriminant direction wood:=Σ1 poolδ, and we use s:= SNR(wid). In experiments, we substitute the OOD-trained logistic-regression direction for the Fisher discriminant direction. Empirically, MCS(wid, wLR ood) MCS(wid, wLDA ood ) in all settings (see Fig. 10 in App. I), and the R2 with AUROC are very close (see Tab. 2 in App. C), so the substitution is largely harmless. 3.2 Background results The proof rests on three classic facts. We state them here and defer the textbook proofs to App. E. Lemma 1 (Covariance decomposition). Under Assumption 1, Σtot := Cov(X) = Σpool + 1 4 δδ. Lemma 2 (Binormal AUROC). Under Assump2(cid:1), where tions 12, AUROC(w)=Φ(cid:0)SNR(w)/ Φ is the standard Gaussian CDF, for any w=0. Lemma 3 (Fishers discriminant). Under Assumption 1, SNR2(w) is maximised on Rd {0} by any nonzero scalar multiple of wood = Σ1 poolδ, with maximum SNR z2 poolδ, where zmax is known as the Fisher distance. max = δΣ1 3 3.3 Closed form for the Mahalanobis cosine The Mahalanobis cosine of an arbitrary direction wid with the Fisher direction wood admits closed form in two scalars: the SNR of wid and the Fisher distance zmax of the task. Theorem 1 (Closed form for MCSΣtot). Under Assumption 1, for any wid with idΣpoolwid > 0 and zmax > 0, MCSΣtot(wid, wood) = zmax (cid:115) 1 + 1 4 z2 max 1 + 1 4 s2 , (3) where := SNR(wid). Moreover, in the largeFisher-distance limit, lim zmax MCSΣtot(s) = 4 + s2 , (4) the softsign function: bounded in (1, 1), nearly linear near s=0, and saturating to 1 as . The proof is direct computation of the three quadratic forms in (1) using Lemma 1 (App. F). Although the derivation uses no objects beyond those already in the LDA literature, the resulting identity has not been written down, likely because the question it answers how MCS of an arbitrary direction with the Fisher direction depends on SNR was not previously connected to probe transfer. The algebra is basic; what matters is the shape. On (zmax, zmax), MCSΣtot(s) is odd, strictly increasing, and satisfies MCSΣtot(zmax)= 1. As zmax, MCSΣtot(s) is exactly softsign shape (Eq. 4; Fig. 2(b)). 3.4 Why AUROC is linear in MCS, with task-independent slope Lemma 2 and Theorem 1 together imply that for fixed OOD task (fixed zmax), both AUROC and MCSΣtot are monotone functions of the signal-tonoise ratio s, tracing parametric curve indexed only by zmax. Two facts make this curve nearlinear law with universal slope. ϕ(s/ (i) Fixed zmax: saturations cancel. Differentiating the parametric curve at any gives: (5) AUROC MCSΣtot = 2) (1 + s2/4)3/2 max + 1/ , (cid:112)2/z2 where ϕ is the standard Gaussian density (App. G). The two s-dependent factors are opposed in s: 2) shrinks (the AUROC sigas grows, ϕ(s/ moid saturating) while (1+s2/4)3/2 grows (the MCS softsign saturating). Their product is therefore much flatter than either factor alone, so the local slope stays close to its central value over the bulk of MCSΣtot (1, 1), as shown in Fig. 2c. The cancellation is not exact: as MCSΣtot 1, the Gaussian factor decays faster than the polynomial grows, and the local slope drops toward 0 (both data and theory at top-right of Fig. 2c flatten). (ii) Per-task slope is universal. The slope in Eq. 5 factors as h(s)g(zmax), where h(s) captures s-dependence and g(zmax)=1/(cid:112)2/z2 max + 1/2 captures task dependence. At zmax>20, which holds in all empirical conditions (Tab. 3), g(zmax) lies within 0.5% of its limit. Therefore, the per-task AUROCMCS curve aligns with the zmax limit curve to within 0.5%. At s=0, this limit curve has central slope h(0) g()=1/ π (App. G). Combined with (i), which establishes that the limit curve is approximately linear, with empirical slopes lying slightly below 1/ π as expected (Fig. 9; App. G), since saturation-tail sampling drags the global slope down from its central value.

sections.title: 4 When does the linearity break down
sections.content: The AUROCMCS linearity holds when: MCS is computed against Σtot, not Σpool; the Fisher distance zmax is large; class proportions are roughly balanced; the OOD probes are close to the optimal Fisher direction. Each ingredient can fail in practice. Fig. 3 shows what each violation does to the linearity; the four cases are quoted at the same axes throughout, and reported R2 are linear fits of OOD AUROC against MCS. (a) Wrong covariance. Using Σpool instead of Σtot turns MCS into s/zmax exactly, so AUROC becomes sigmoid in MCS rather than line (see App. for derivation). On the main experiment data, the linear fit R2 drops from 0.98 to 0.83. (b) Non-Fisher probe. The theory does not hold if OOD probes deviate too much from the optimal Fisher directions, for example, for the commonly used difference of means probe (Marks and Tegmark, 2023). On the same empirical data, the 4 Figure 3: Failure modes. Each panel illustrates violation of an assumption in 3, and the linearity breaks. diffmean probe gives markedly lower R2 of 0.79. This delimits the law: it predicts generalization for Fisher-style probes (LR, LDA, shrinkage variants), not for diffmean-style probes. (c) Small Fisher distance. For small zmax, the slope of the MCS formula does not saturate, so each task is in its own near-linear regime with its own slope. Synthetic Gaussian data at zmax {0.1, 0.5, 1, 2} exhibits clear fan of per-group slopes spanning large range. The LLM data is unaffected because zmax>20 on every tasks (see Tab. 3; App. C). The linear fit is tight for each group, but taken together, the R2 drops to 0.666. (d) Class imbalance. The balanced-class factor 1 4 in the MCS formula generalises to π(1 π), so the AUROCMCS slope steepens as π moves away from 1 2 (App. H). Synthetic data at π {0.5, 0.1, 0.02, 0.004} shows monotone steepening. The R2 drops to 0.84 in this case.

sections.title: 5 Discussion
sections.content: Our results suggest that Mahalanobis cosine similarity is theoretically sound alternative to standard Euclidean cosine similarity (consistent with recent arguments for non-Euclidean inner products (Park et al., 2024)). This also points to broader research direction: many interpretability methods that currently rely on cosine similarity may benefit from the Mahalanobis alternative (steering-vector comparison, SAE feature alignment, concept-direction clustering, data filtering, data attribution, etc.). Future work can use unsupervised methods like CCS (Burns et al., 2023) for the OOD direction, enabling fully label-free prediction of probe generalization.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Our results have several limitations. First, computing MCΣtot requires estimating wood and Σtot from labeled OOD data; the method is trainingfree for the candidate probe wid but not label-free for the target distribution. Second, the theory is calibrated to Fisher-style probes (LR, LDA); for difference-of-means probes the linearity degrades markedly (Fig. 3b), and closed form for nonFisher references remains open. Third, our evaluation is confined to binary classification on residualstream features of autoregressive LLMs; multiclass probes, attention/MLP features, and non-LLM architectures are untested. Fourth, the law is fundamentally approximate: the per-task total slope is close to 1/ π and the local slope goes to 0 at the extreme. Finally, the theory assumes per-class projection Gaussianity; while this holds empirically for most directions  (Fig. 7)  and the linearity survives clear violations in simulation  (Fig. 8)  , formal guarantees do not extend to heavy-tailed projections. Risks. We do not foresee risks specific to this work; it is theoretical analysis of an existing similarity measure.

sections.title: A Extended related works
sections.content: Linear probes and their generalization. Linear probes are standard interpretability tool (Alain and Bengio, 2016; Belinkov, 2022), used to read off concepts such as truthfulness (Marks and Tegmark, 2023), but often degrade under distribution shift (Hewitt and Liang, 2019; Levinstein and Herrmann, 2024; Orgad et al., 2025). Prior work predicts linear-classifier generalization from geometric properties of the representationkernel-target alignment (Cristianini et al., 2001), spectral rank (Garrido et al., 2023), and neural-collapse statistics (Papyan et al., 2020; Galanti et al., 2021)but these score whole representations rather than individual probe directions. The near-linear AUROC MCS relationship we explain was reported empirically by Ying et al. (2026) on truthfulness datasets; we prove it, characterize when it fails, and verify it across models, layers, and domains. Classical statistical machinery. Our theory composes three textbook results: Fishers linear discriminant (Fisher, 1936), the binormal AUROC formula (Bamber, 1975; Green et al., 1966; Hanley and McNeil, 1982), and the withinbetween covariance decomposition underlying MANOVA (Wilks, 1932; Chatfield, 2018). None is novel in isolation; the contribution lies in composing them into closed-form quantity computable from candidate probe direction together with two target-task moments. Agreement-on-the-Line. The closest theoretical neighbor is the Agreement-on-the-Line framework (Miller et al., 2021; Baek et al., 2022, 2025), which proves linear ID-to-OOD relationship for probitscaled accuracy and agreement rates between linear classifiers under Gaussian data. Baek et al. (2025) parameterize this relationship by similarity that is algebraically the Mahalanobis cosine of Eq. (1). We differ in (i) targeting threshold-free AUROC, which admits the closed form of Theorem 1; (ii) predicting OOD performance from single geometric quantity against the OOD Fisher direction, with no ID baseline; and (iii) identifying the uniπ from the AUROCMCS versal slope around 1/ saturation cancellation, which has no analog in the AOL line. Mahalanobis cosine as similarity for direction comparison. The most direct precursor of our setup is Bolme et al. (2003), who introduced "Mahalanobis cosine" as similarity measure in PCA-whitened face-recognition pipelines. Iacovacci et al. (2020) reused the same expression for inferring omics networks. Both works treat MCS as similarity score to be evaluated empirically against task performance; neither expresses MCS in closed form as function of probe SNR and task Fisher distance, and neither links it to AUROC. Our contribution in 3 is to make precise why MCS works as direction-comparison similarity for classification in closed form (Theorem 1) whose composition with the binormal AUROC is task-calibrated and predicts held-out performance. Non-Euclidean geometry of LLM representations. Park et al. (2024) also argue against Euclidean cosine on LLM representation space and propose "causal inner product" derived from counterfactual considerations on the unembedding space. Their specific instantiation whitens by the covariance of unembedding token vectors, single global matrix per model. Our Σtot is per-task activation covariance computed on the OOD train data, and operates in the residual-stream embedding space rather than the unembedding space. Mahalanobis distance in OOD detection and transferability. Mahalanobis distances of test points are widely used as out-of-distribution scores (Lee et al., 2018; Ren et al., 2021), but they score raw inputs rather than candidate probe directions. separate transferability-metrics literature (LEEP, LogME, H-score, GBC, NCTI, Task2Vec; e.g. You et al., 2021; Pándy et al., 2022) predicts downstream linear-probe performance from frozen features, but operates on whole representations and typically requires target-side labels. To our knowledge, no prior method predicts the held-out AUROC of specific probe direction in closed form from two target-task moments.

sections.title: B Experimental details
sections.content: B.1 Computational resources. All experiments are done on local L40S and A40. Experiments require 4 GPUs to run for about 10 hours. We use Huggingface and NNsight to extract activations (Wolf et al., 2020; Fiotto-Kaufman et al., 2024). The logistic regression probe is implemented with Pedregosa et al. (2011). B.2 Model and probe details Llama-3.3-70B, Models. We use Llama-3.1-8B, and Qwen-2.5-7B (Grattafiori 8 et al., 2024; Qwen et al., 2025). We use layer 15 and layer 19 for Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen-2.5-7B following Ying et al. (2026). B.3 Dataset details Truthfulness datasets. We use ten total truthfulness datasets: five fundamental truth types from the FLEED dataset (definitional, empirical, logical, fictional, and ethical truth) (8k samples), sycophantic lying (4k samples), expectation inverted lying (600 sample), and three on-policy deception datasets (roleplay (371 samples), insider trading (1.5k samples), and sandbagging (1k samples)) (Scheurer et al., 2023; Benton et al., 2024; Goldowsky-Dill et al., 2025; Ying et al., 2026). For Qwen-2.5-7B, we dont have the three on-policy deception datasets. Therefore, it is evaluated only on 7 datasets. Gender classification datasets. We use six gender classification datasets: PAN17 (Martinc et al., 2017), CrowdFlower Twitter gender classification (Eight, 2016), HappyDB (Asai et al., 2018), BiosBias (De-Arteaga et al., 2019), WinoBias (Zhao et al., 2018), and GAP (Webster et al., 2018). For datasets larger than 4000 samples, we randomly subsample to 4000 samples. Therefore, GAP has 2,000 samples, PAN17 randomly combines tweets from the same user and has 2,400 samples, WinoBias has 1,584 samples, and the rest has 4,000 samples each. General NLP benchmarks. We use eight classic NLP QA datasets: sentiment classification (IMDB (Maas et al., 2011) and Amazon (McAuley and Leskovec, 2013)), topic classification (AG-News (Zhang et al., 2015) and DBpedia-14 (Lehmann et al., 2015)), NLI (RTE (Wang et al., 2018)), question answering (BoolQ (Clark et al., 2019)), and common sense reasoning (Bisk et al., 2020)). We also subsample large datasets to 4,000 samples. Therefore, PIQA has 3,674 samples, RTE has 552 samples, and the rest has 4,000 samples each. License. All datasets used in this work are publicly available and applied here only for noncommercial academic evaluation, consistent with the terms set by their original authors. Explicit licenses, where available, are: MIT (De-Arteaga et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2018), Apache 2.0 (Webster et al., 2018), CC BY-SA 3.0 (Lehmann et al., 2015; Clark et al., 2019), Academic Free License v3.0 (Bisk et al., 2020), and CC BY 4.0 (Eight, 2016; Scheurer et al., 2023). The remaining datasets are released under research-use terms specified in their associated publications. All data is English-language.

sections.title: C Additional empirical results
sections.content: Cross-domain generalization performance. As shown in Fig 4, we observe rich cross-domain generalization patterns across all eight conditions. MCS and ECS against AUROC. Tab. 1 summarizes the performance of MCS and ECS on predicting OOD AUROC. We show the scatter plots for all eight conditions in Fig 5. We observe right crossdomain generalization patterns across all eight conditions. Verification of the theory across conditions. We show the theory against empirical data across all eight conditions in Fig. 6. In all conditions, the theoretical prediction tracks the empirical data very well. ood instead of wLR ood) vs. MCSΣtot(wid, wLDA Using wLDA ood for the empirical results. We show the performance of using MCSΣtot(wid, wLR ood ) to predict the OOD generalization performance in Tab. 2. Using MCSΣtot(wid, wLDA ood ) is better than MCSΣtot(wid, wLR ood) in all conditions, as expected. But the difference is small (< 0.05). Empirical AUROCMCS slopes are consistent with theory. As shown in Fig. 9, the empirically fitted slopes across 8 conditions are consistent with the theoretical prediction from App. G. Measuring Gaussianity of the empirical data. As shown in Fig. 7, most of the empirical skewness and kurtosis are small across all conditions, all probe directions, and all test data distributions. Specifically, 73% of the skewness is within 0.5 and 79% of the kurtosis is within 1. Simulating non-Gaussian distributions. As shown in Fig. 8, the AUROCMCS R2 is extremely high in simulations, even for distributions that are deliberately designed to violate the projectionGaussianity assumption. Empirical zmax across all datasets. As shown in Tab. 3, all zmax across all conditions and datasets exceed the saturation threshold zmax 20 at which the slopes lie within 0.5% of their limits (App. G), pinning all per-task AUROCMCS slopes to universal slope. We probe two axes of robustness: (i) the reference direction, comparing logistic regression (LR) MCSΣtot(wid, wLR ood) against the theoretically motivated LDA direction MCSΣtot(wid, wLDA ood ), and the LDA version dominates the LR version, but the difference is small; (ii) the shrinkage estimator for Σtot, comparing the full covariance against per-coordinate diagonal approximation (MCStot,diag(wid, wLR ood)) and LedoitWolf shrinkage (MCStot,LW (wid, wLR ood)). LedoitWolf shrinkage is very similar to the full covariance we used, while the diagonal covariance is significantly worse. sections.title: D AI Usage
sections.content: We use AI assistants to assist with writing paper drafts, coding for experiments, and writing proofs. Robustness to the Σtot estimator. Tab. 2 reports the AUROCMCS linear-fit R2 under three estimators of Σtot: the full sample covariance, LedoitWolf shrinkage, and per-coordinate diagonal approximation. LedoitWolf is essentially indistinguishable from the full covariance across all conditions, so the headline result is not an artifact of particular regularization choice. The diagonal approximation, by contrast, degrades sharply: most strikingly on general NLP (R2=0.002 vs. 0.936), confirming that off-diagonal covariance structure is essential to MCS and that the linearity is not recoverable from per-coordinate variances alone. 10 Figure 4: Cross-domain generalization performance for all eight conditions across models, layers, and concept domains. We observe rich cross-domain generalization patterns across all eight conditions. 11 Figure 5: MCS and ECS against AUROC across conditions. We observe strong linear relationship between MCS and AUROC for all eight conditions across models, layers, and concept domains, while the relationship between ECS and AUROC is much weaker. 12 Figure 6: Empirical verification of the theory. The theory predicts the empirical data well across conditions. Figure 7: Most directions are largely Gaussian across all conditions. Across all conditions, all probe directions, and all test data distributions, most samples are largely Gaussian. Some samples have notably high kurtosis, which is mostly attributed to the sycophantic lying dataset. Figure 8: The strong linearity between AUROC and MCS still holds for non-Gaussian distributions. On deliberated constructed distributions where the projection-Gaussianity assumption is broken, the relationship between AUROC and MCS is still linear.

sections.title: H Failure mode details
sections.content: zmax 4 6 8 20 g(zmax)/g() 0.894 0.949 0.970 0. This appendix collects the formal statements behind the failure modes summarised in 4. Every empirical task has zmax > 20 (Tab. 3), so each tasks AUROCMCS curve coincides with the zmax limit curve to within 0.5% on the slope. Shape factor h(s) and central slope. h(s) combines ϕ(s/ 2) (strictly decreasing in s) with (1+s2/4)3/2 (strictly increasing in s). Their product is far flatter than either factor alone this is the saturation cancellation behind (i): as zmax, the AUROC sigmoid saturates (shrinking ϕ) at the same time as the MCS softsign saturates (growing (1 + s2/4)3/2), and the two effects largely offset. At the centre, h(0) = 1/ 2π, giving the universal central slope h(0) g() = 2 2π = 1 π 0.564. The cancellation is not exact: h(s) g() peaks at = 2 with value 0.629, then decays toward 0 as zmax, which is why R2 < 1 in the empirical AUROCMCS fits. H.1 Use Σpool metric instead of Σtot We have: idΣpoolwood = idδ = v; idΣpoolwid = and oodΣpoolwood = δΣ1 poolδ = z2 max v/( Substituting into (1) gives zmax) = s/zmax. Therefore, MCSΣpool is just rescaled s. This will not cancel out the S-shaped between OOD AUROC and s, and thus will not be linear to OOD AUROC. H.2 Small Fisher distance When zmax is small (zmax 2), the slope around 0 κ(zmax) is far below its limit and varies rapidly with zmax: κ(1) 0.25 vs. κ(2) 0.40 vs. κ(3) 0.47. collection of test sets with heterogeneous small-zmax tasks therefore does not collapse onto single linear fit; each task has its own 15 slope, producing fan rather than line. On synthetic data with zmax controlled to {0.1, 0.5, 1, 2} (Fig. 3c), we observe exactly this fan, and the global linear R2 drops to 0.666. This failure mode is not engaged on our LLM datasets because every task has zmax > 20, inside the saturation regime (see Tab. 3; App. C). H.3 Strong class imbalance For class prior π, we redefine the pooled vvariance as: Σpool := (1 π)Σ0 + πΣ1 Then Lemma 1 generalises to Σtot = Σpool + π(1 π) δδ , and Theorem 1 becomes MCSΣtot(wid, wood)= (cid:115) zmax 1 + π(1 π)z2 1 + π(1 π)s2 , max preserving the softsign-cancels-sigmoid story but with re-scaled saturation point and slope. Strong imbalance (e.g., π = 0.01) shrinks the π(1 π) factor by 25 versus the balanced case, weakening the saturation cancellation in MCS and steepening the AUROCMCS slope. Our probe datasets are all approximately balanced, so this mode is not engaged in our experiments, but it would matter for naturally imbalanced applications such as rareevent detection.

sections.title: I Empirical alignment of LR and LDA
sections.content: reference directions poolδ as the OOD reference. The theory in 3 uses the Fisher direction wLDA ood = Σ1 In the empirical experiments, we replace it with the OOD-trained logistic-regression direction wLR ood, on the grounds that for balanced binary tasks with well-separated classes the two directions are nearly proportional. This appendix quantifies the substitution. Setup. For each of datasets used in 2, on the train half of stratified 50/50 split, we compute ood = (Σpool + λI)1δ (i) the Fisher direction wLDA with λ = 106, and (ii) the LR direction wLR ood. We measure the correlation between MC(wLR id , wLR ood) and MC(wLR id , wLDA ood ). Result. As shown in Fig. 10, across all settings (across models, layers, and concept domains), the pairwise Pearson correlation between MCSΣtot(wid, wLR ood ) is ood) and MCSΣtot(wid, wLDA id , wLR ood) vs. MC(wLR Figure 10: MC(wLR ood ). The two MCs correlate strongly across all eight conditions, explaining why substituting the LDA direction with the LR direction in our empirical experiments does not affect the observed linearity. id , wLDA > 0.99, with mean (wid) < 0.07. Substituting wLR ood in the headline regression of 2 changes the linear-fit R2 by less than 1%. ood for wLDA Why this works. For balanced binary classification with classes that are jointly well-modelled the LR by Gaussians with shared covariance, maximum-likelihood direction and the Fisher direction coincide up to scaling (Efron, 1975; Hastie, 2009). Real activations only approximately satisfy these assumptions, but all of our probe datasets are easy ID AUROC exceeds 0.95 in every case so the residual mismatch between wLR ood and wLDA ood is small in cosine. We expect larger mismatch on harder, less-separable tasks; characterising that regime is useful direction for future work.
